Mathematics.teks_description: Write the quadratic function given three specified points in the plane
Mathematics.prompt: This topic covers advanced quadratic functions for 10th grade Mathematics students (TEKS 2A.4(A)). Students should write quadratic functions given three specified points in the plane. Method: substitute each point (x, y) into y = ax^2 + bx + c to create a system of three equations with three unknowns (a, b, c), then solve using elimination, substitution, or matrices. Key concepts: three non-collinear points uniquely determine a parabola, quadratic regression uses technology for best-fit parabolas through data, vertex form reveals maximum/minimum and axis of symmetry, standard form reveals y-intercept. Advanced topics: solving systems involving quadratic equations, analyzing intersections of quadratic and linear functions (0, 1, or 2 solutions), completing the square for any quadratic. Vocabulary: quadratic function, parabola, system of equations, coefficient, standard form, vertex form, factored form, leading coefficient, discriminant, roots, zeros.

Mathematics.teks_description: Add, subtract, and multiply polynomials; determine linear and quadratic factors
Mathematics.prompt: This topic covers polynomial functions for 10th grade Mathematics students (TEKS 2A.7(B)). Students should add, subtract, and multiply polynomials and determine linear and quadratic factors. Operations: add/subtract by combining like terms; multiply using distributive property or area model. Factoring: find GCF, use grouping, identify special patterns (difference of squares, sum/difference of cubes, perfect square trinomials). Graphs: degree determines maximum number of turning points (n-1) and x-intercepts (n). End behavior: determined by leading coefficient and degree — even degree with positive leading coefficient: both ends up; odd degree with positive leading coefficient: left down, right up. Key concepts: zeros/roots are x-intercepts, multiplicity of zeros affects graph behavior (odd multiplicity crosses, even multiplicity touches), Fundamental Theorem of Algebra (degree n polynomial has exactly n roots counting multiplicity and complex). Vocabulary: polynomial, degree, leading coefficient, end behavior, zero, root, multiplicity, turning point, factor, factor theorem, remainder theorem.

Mathematics.teks_description: Determine asymptotic restrictions and represent domain/range using notation and set theory
Mathematics.prompt: This topic covers rational functions for 10th grade Mathematics students (TEKS 2A.6(K)). Students should determine asymptotic restrictions and represent domain and range using interval notation and set theory. Rational function: f(x) = p(x)/q(x) where p and q are polynomials. Domain: all real numbers except where denominator equals zero. Vertical asymptotes: occur at x-values where denominator = 0 and numerator is not 0 (function approaches infinity). Horizontal asymptotes: determined by comparing degrees of numerator and denominator — same degree: ratio of leading coefficients; numerator degree less: y = 0; numerator degree greater: no horizontal asymptote (may have oblique). Holes: occur where both numerator and denominator equal zero (common factor). Key concepts: graphing rational functions using transformations of y = 1/x, identifying intercepts, analyzing behavior near asymptotes, representing domain and range using set-builder notation {x | x is not equal to a} and interval notation. Vocabulary: rational function, asymptote, vertical asymptote, horizontal asymptote, oblique asymptote, hole, discontinuity, domain, range, interval notation, set-builder notation.

Mathematics.teks_description: Determine transformation effects on square root functions with parameter variations
Mathematics.prompt: This topic covers radical functions for 10th grade Mathematics students (TEKS 2A.4(C)). Students should determine transformation effects on square root functions with parameter variations. Parent function: f(x) = sqrt(x); domain x >= 0, range y >= 0; graph starts at origin and increases. Transformations: a*sqrt(x) (vertical stretch/compression and reflection), sqrt(x - h) + k (horizontal shift h, vertical shift k), sqrt(bx) (horizontal stretch/compression). Key features: starting point (vertex) at (h, k) for f(x) = a*sqrt(x - h) + k; domain x >= h; range depends on a and k. Solving radical equations: isolate the radical, square both sides, solve, and always check for extraneous solutions (squaring can introduce invalid answers). Graphing: plot the starting point, use transformations, plot additional points. Key concepts: domain restrictions in radical functions, inverse relationship between quadratic and square root functions, compositions involving radical functions. Vocabulary: radical function, square root, domain, range, transformation, starting point, extraneous solution, inverse function, principal root, index, radicand.

Mathematics.teks_description: Determine transformation effects on exponential and logarithmic graphs
Mathematics.prompt: This topic covers exponential and logarithmic functions for 10th grade Mathematics students (TEKS 2A.5(A)). Students should determine transformation effects on exponential and logarithmic graphs. Exponential parent: f(x) = b^x (b > 0, b is not 1); domain all reals, range y > 0, horizontal asymptote y = 0, passes through (0,1). Logarithmic parent: f(x) = log_b(x); domain x > 0, range all reals, vertical asymptote x = 0, passes through (1,0). These are inverse functions: if y = b^x then x = log_b(y). Transformations: vertical shifts, horizontal shifts, reflections, stretches/compressions follow same rules as other functions. Key features after transformation: shifted asymptotes, changed domain/range, new intercepts. Natural exponential e ≈ 2.718; natural logarithm ln(x) = log_e(x). Common logarithm log(x) = log_10(x). Vocabulary: exponential function, logarithmic function, base, asymptote, inverse function, natural logarithm, common logarithm, transformation, growth, decay, domain, range, e.

Mathematics.teks_description: Rewrite exponential equations as logarithmic equations and vice versa
Mathematics.prompt: This topic covers properties of logarithms for 10th grade Mathematics students (TEKS 2A.5(C)). Students should rewrite exponential equations as logarithmic equations and vice versa. Conversion: b^y = x is equivalent to log_b(x) = y. Properties of logarithms: product rule log_b(MN) = log_b(M) + log_b(N), quotient rule log_b(M/N) = log_b(M) - log_b(N), power rule log_b(M^p) = p × log_b(M). Special values: log_b(1) = 0 (since b^0 = 1), log_b(b) = 1 (since b^1 = b). Change of base formula: log_b(x) = log(x)/log(b) = ln(x)/ln(b), essential for calculator use. Key concepts: logarithms are exponents, using properties to expand or condense logarithmic expressions, solving equations by converting between exponential and logarithmic forms. Applications: pH calculations, Richter scale, decibel scale, doubling time, half-life. Vocabulary: logarithm, base, exponent, product rule, quotient rule, power rule, change of base, common logarithm, natural logarithm, expand, condense, exponential form, logarithmic form.

Mathematics.teks_description: Solve exponential equations of the form y=ab^x where a is a nonzero real number
Mathematics.prompt: This topic covers solving exponential and logarithmic equations for 10th grade Mathematics students (TEKS 2A.5(D)). Students should solve exponential equations of the form y = ab^x. Methods for exponential equations: if bases can be made equal, set exponents equal (same base method); otherwise, take logarithm of both sides and use properties to solve. Methods for logarithmic equations: convert to exponential form, use properties to combine logarithms, then solve. Key steps: isolate the exponential or logarithmic expression, apply appropriate operation (log or exponentiation), solve for variable, check solution (logarithm of negative number or zero is undefined). Applications: compound interest (finding time to reach a target), population growth, radioactive decay (finding half-life or time), cooling/heating curves. Key equations: continuous growth/decay A = Pe^(rt), compound interest A = P(1 + r/n)^(nt). Vocabulary: exponential equation, logarithmic equation, isolate, extraneous solution, compound interest, continuous growth, half-life, doubling time.

Mathematics.teks_description: Determine the relationship between the unit circle and the definition of a periodic function
Mathematics.prompt: This topic covers trigonometric functions for 10th grade Mathematics students (TEKS P.4(A)). Students should understand the relationship between the unit circle and periodic functions. Unit circle: circle with radius 1 centered at origin; point on circle at angle θ has coordinates (cos θ, sin θ). Key angles: 0°, 30°, 45°, 60°, 90° and their equivalents in all quadrants; radians conversion: degrees × π/180. Graphs of trig functions: sine and cosine have amplitude 1, period 2π, range [-1,1]; tangent has period π, vertical asymptotes, range all reals. Transformations: y = A sin(B(x - C)) + D where |A| = amplitude, 2π/|B| = period, C = phase shift, D = vertical shift. Key concepts: periodic functions repeat at regular intervals, reference angles, ASTC rule (All Students Take Calculus) for sign of trig functions by quadrant, reciprocal functions (csc, sec, cot). Vocabulary: unit circle, radian, degree, sine, cosine, tangent, period, amplitude, phase shift, vertical shift, periodic function, reference angle, quadrant, reciprocal.

Mathematics.teks_description: Use trigonometric identities including reciprocal, quotient, Pythagorean, cofunctions, even/odd
Mathematics.prompt: This topic covers trigonometric identities for 10th grade Mathematics students (TEKS P.5(M)). Students should use fundamental trigonometric identities to simplify expressions and verify equations. Reciprocal identities: csc θ = 1/sin θ, sec θ = 1/cos θ, cot θ = 1/tan θ. Quotient identities: tan θ = sin θ/cos θ, cot θ = cos θ/sin θ. Pythagorean identities: sin²θ + cos²θ = 1, tan²θ + 1 = sec²θ, 1 + cot²θ = csc²θ. Even-odd identities: cos(-θ) = cos θ (even), sin(-θ) = -sin θ (odd), tan(-θ) = -tan θ (odd). Cofunction identities: sin(π/2 - θ) = cos θ, etc. Strategies for verifying identities: work on one side only (usually the more complex side), convert everything to sine and cosine, factor, find common denominators, multiply by conjugates. Key concepts: an identity is true for all values in the domain, verifying vs. solving equations. Vocabulary: identity, reciprocal, quotient, Pythagorean identity, cofunction, even function, odd function, simplify, verify, expression.

Mathematics.teks_description: Generate and solve trigonometric equations in mathematical and real-world problems
Mathematics.prompt: This topic covers solving trigonometric equations for 10th grade Mathematics students (TEKS P.5(N)). Students should generate and solve trigonometric equations in mathematical and real-world problems. Key concepts: solving trig equations is similar to solving algebraic equations but solutions repeat due to periodicity. General solutions include +2πn (or +360°n) for sine and cosine, +πn (or +180°n) for tangent. Methods: isolate the trig function, find reference angle, identify all solutions in [0, 2π), add multiples of the period for general solution. For equations like 2sin²x - 1 = 0: treat as quadratic (let u = sin x), solve for u, then find x. Using identities: substitute using Pythagorean or double-angle identities to get equation in one trig function. Real-world applications: modeling periodic phenomena (tides, daylight hours, sound waves, temperature). Vocabulary: trigonometric equation, general solution, reference angle, period, quadratic form, inverse trig function, principal value, periodic.

Mathematics.teks_description: Apply theorems about angles, radii, chords, tangents, and secants of circles
Mathematics.prompt: This topic covers geometry for 10th grade Mathematics students (TEKS G.12(A)). Students should apply theorems about circles including angles, radii, chords, tangents, and secants. Circle theorems: central angle equals intercepted arc; inscribed angle equals half the intercepted arc; tangent is perpendicular to radius at point of tangency; two tangents from external point are equal in length. Chord theorems: perpendicular from center bisects chord; equal chords are equidistant from center. Secant-tangent relationships: power of a point theorems for intersecting chords, secants from external point, and tangent-secant from external point. Arc length: s = rθ (radians) or s = (θ/360)2πr. Sector area: A = ½r²θ (radians) or A = (θ/360)πr². Polygons: sum of interior angles = (n-2)180°; regular polygon interior angle = (n-2)180°/n. Vocabulary: circle, radius, diameter, chord, tangent, secant, arc, central angle, inscribed angle, intercepted arc, sector, segment, polygon, regular polygon, inscribed, circumscribed.

Mathematics.teks_description: Prove triangle congruence using SAS, ASA, SSS, AAS, and HL conditions
Mathematics.prompt: This topic covers proofs in geometry for 10th grade Mathematics students (TEKS G.6(B)). Students should prove triangle congruence using SAS, ASA, SSS, AAS, and HL conditions. Triangle congruence postulates/theorems: SSS (three sides equal), SAS (two sides and included angle), ASA (two angles and included side), AAS (two angles and non-included side), HL (hypotenuse-leg for right triangles). NOT valid: SSA/ASS (ambiguous case). Proof formats: two-column proof (statements and reasons), paragraph proof, flow proof. Key proof strategies: mark given information on diagram, identify shared sides or vertical angles, use properties (reflexive, symmetric, transitive), apply definitions and postulates. CPCTC: Corresponding Parts of Congruent Triangles are Congruent — used after proving triangles congruent to show specific parts are equal. Triangle similarity: AA (two angles), SAS similarity (proportional sides with included angle), SSS similarity (all sides proportional). Vocabulary: congruent, similar, postulate, theorem, proof, corresponding parts, CPCTC, reflexive property, transitive property, included angle, included side, hypotenuse, leg.

Mathematics.teks_description: Derive and use distance, slope, and midpoint formulas to verify geometric properties
Mathematics.prompt: This topic covers coordinate geometry for 10th grade Mathematics students (TEKS G.2(B)). Students should derive and use distance, slope, and midpoint formulas to verify geometric properties. Distance formula: d = sqrt((x2-x1)^2 + (y2-y1)^2), derived from Pythagorean theorem. Midpoint formula: M = ((x1+x2)/2, (y1+y2)/2). Slope: m = (y2-y1)/(x2-x1). Applications: prove triangles are isosceles (equal side lengths), right (perpendicular slopes, product = -1), equilateral; prove quadrilaterals are parallelograms (opposite sides equal and parallel), rectangles (parallelogram with right angles), rhombi (parallelogram with equal sides), squares (rectangle and rhombus). Equation of circle: (x-h)^2 + (y-k)^2 = r^2 with center (h,k) and radius r. Proving geometric relationships algebraically using coordinates is called coordinate proof or analytic geometry. Vocabulary: coordinate plane, distance formula, midpoint formula, slope, parallel, perpendicular, coordinate proof, analytic geometry, quadrilateral, diagonal, verify.

Mathematics.teks_description: Use the characteristics of an ellipse to write the equation of an ellipse with center (h,k)
Mathematics.prompt: This topic covers conic sections for 10th grade Mathematics students (TEKS P.3(H)). Students should use characteristics of ellipses to write equations with center (h,k). Conic sections: curves formed by intersecting a cone with a plane. Circle: (x-h)^2 + (y-k)^2 = r^2. Ellipse: (x-h)^2/a^2 + (y-k)^2/b^2 = 1 (horizontal major axis if a > b). Key features of ellipse: center (h,k), vertices (endpoints of major axis), co-vertices (endpoints of minor axis), foci (c^2 = a^2 - b^2). Parabola: (y-k) = a(x-h)^2 or (x-h) = a(y-k)^2; vertex at (h,k), focus, directrix. Hyperbola: (x-h)^2/a^2 - (y-k)^2/b^2 = 1; center, vertices, foci (c^2 = a^2 + b^2), asymptotes. Key concepts: identifying conic type from equation, converting general form to standard form by completing the square, eccentricity. Vocabulary: conic section, ellipse, parabola, hyperbola, circle, center, vertex, focus, directrix, major axis, minor axis, asymptote, eccentricity, standard form.

Mathematics.teks_description: Apply conditional probability in contextual problems
Mathematics.prompt: This topic covers probability for 10th grade Mathematics students (TEKS G.13(D)). Students should apply conditional probability in contextual problems. Conditional probability: P(A|B) = P(A and B)/P(B), the probability of A given B has occurred. Independence: events A and B are independent if P(A|B) = P(A), equivalently P(A and B) = P(A) × P(B). Multiplication rule: P(A and B) = P(A) × P(B|A) for dependent events; P(A and B) = P(A) × P(B) for independent events. Addition rule: P(A or B) = P(A) + P(B) - P(A and B); for mutually exclusive events: P(A or B) = P(A) + P(B). Two-way frequency tables: organize data to calculate marginal, joint, and conditional probabilities. Key concepts: testing independence using probability calculations, Bayes' theorem introduction, tree diagrams for sequential events. Vocabulary: conditional probability, independence, dependent events, mutually exclusive, two-way table, joint probability, marginal probability, tree diagram, sample space, complement.

Mathematics.teks_description: Evaluate the benefits of different sampling techniques including random and convenience sampling
Mathematics.prompt: This topic covers statistics for 10th grade Mathematics students (TEKS S.2(A)). Students should evaluate benefits of different sampling techniques. Sampling methods: simple random sample (every member has equal chance), stratified random sample (population divided into subgroups, random sample from each), systematic sample (every nth member), convenience sample (easily accessible, biased), cluster sample (randomly select groups, survey all in chosen groups). Bias: systematic error in sampling; types include response bias, nonresponse bias, selection bias, question wording bias. Distributions: normal distribution (bell-shaped, symmetric, 68-95-99.7 rule), skewed distributions (left or right). Measures: mean, median, mode, range, standard deviation, IQR, z-scores (z = (x - μ)/σ). Sampling distributions: distribution of sample statistics; Central Limit Theorem states sampling distribution of mean approaches normal as n increases. Vocabulary: population, sample, sampling method, bias, random, stratified, systematic, normal distribution, standard deviation, z-score, sampling distribution, Central Limit Theorem, parameter, statistic.

Mathematics.teks_description: Evaluate finite sums and geometric series, when possible, written in sigma notation
Mathematics.prompt: This topic covers sequences and series for 10th grade Mathematics students (TEKS P.5(A)). Students should evaluate finite sums and geometric series written in sigma notation. Sigma notation: Σ represents summation; includes index variable, lower and upper bounds, and expression to sum. Arithmetic series: sum of arithmetic sequence; Sn = n/2 × (a1 + an) = n/2 × (2a1 + (n-1)d). Geometric series: sum of geometric sequence; finite Sn = a1(1 - r^n)/(1 - r) for r not equal 1; infinite S = a1/(1 - r) when |r| < 1 (convergent). Key concepts: writing series in sigma notation, evaluating sums by formula or expanding, recognizing arithmetic vs. geometric patterns, determining convergence of infinite geometric series. Telescoping series: terms cancel leaving only first and last. Applications: compound interest, annuities, bouncing ball distance, repeating decimals as infinite geometric series. Vocabulary: series, sequence, sigma notation, summation, arithmetic series, geometric series, partial sum, convergent, divergent, index, common difference, common ratio, infinite series.

Mathematics.teks_description: Use vectors to model situations involving magnitude and direction
Mathematics.prompt: This topic covers vectors for 10th grade Mathematics students (TEKS P.4(I)). Students should use vectors to model situations involving magnitude and direction. Vector basics: vectors have both magnitude (length) and direction; represented as arrows or ordered pairs <a, b> or in component form ai + bj. Vector operations: addition (tip-to-tail or component-wise), subtraction, scalar multiplication (changes magnitude, may reverse direction). Magnitude: |v| = sqrt(a^2 + b^2). Direction angle: θ = tan^(-1)(b/a), adjusted for correct quadrant. Unit vector: vector with magnitude 1; u = v/|v|. Dot product: u · v = |u||v|cos θ = u1v1 + u2v2; result is scalar; used to find angle between vectors. Vectors are perpendicular (orthogonal) when dot product = 0. Applications: force problems (resultant force), velocity (airplane with wind), displacement, navigation. Key concepts: component form, resolving vectors into horizontal and vertical components, equilibrium (sum of forces = zero vector). Vocabulary: vector, scalar, magnitude, direction, component, unit vector, resultant, dot product, orthogonal, equilibrium, displacement, force, velocity.

Mathematics.teks_description: Solve three-variable linear systems using Gaussian elimination, matrices, or substitution
Mathematics.prompt: This topic covers matrices for 10th grade Mathematics students (TEKS 2A.3(B)). Students should solve three-variable linear systems using Gaussian elimination, matrices, or substitution. Matrix basics: rectangular array of numbers with rows and columns; dimensions given as m × n. Operations: addition/subtraction (same dimensions, element-wise), scalar multiplication (multiply all elements), matrix multiplication (row × column, resulting dimensions m × p from m×n times n×p). Solving systems with matrices: write augmented matrix [A|b], perform row operations (swap rows, multiply row by nonzero constant, add multiple of one row to another) to reach row echelon form (Gaussian elimination) or reduced row echelon form (Gauss-Jordan). Types of solutions: unique (one solution), no solution (inconsistent, contradictory row), infinitely many (dependent, free variable). Key concepts: determinants, inverse matrices (A^(-1) exists when det(A) is not 0, solution x = A^(-1)b), Cramer's rule. Vocabulary: matrix, dimension, element, entry, augmented matrix, row operation, echelon form, Gaussian elimination, determinant, inverse matrix, identity matrix, singular matrix, system of equations.

Mathematics.teks_description: Add, subtract, and multiply complex numbers
Mathematics.prompt: This topic covers complex numbers for 10th grade Mathematics students (TEKS 2A.7(A)). Students should add, subtract, and multiply complex numbers. Complex number: z = a + bi, where a is the real part, b is the imaginary part, and i = sqrt(-1) so i^2 = -1. Operations: addition (a+bi) + (c+di) = (a+c) + (b+d)i, subtraction similarly, multiplication using FOIL and i^2 = -1: (a+bi)(c+di) = (ac-bd) + (ad+bc)i. Complex conjugate: z-bar = a - bi; product z × z-bar = a^2 + b^2 (always real and non-negative). Division: multiply numerator and denominator by conjugate of denominator. Powers of i: cycle every 4 (i^1 = i, i^2 = -1, i^3 = -i, i^4 = 1). Key concepts: complex numbers extend the real number system, every quadratic equation has solutions in the complex numbers (Fundamental Theorem of Algebra), discriminant negative means complex (non-real) solutions in conjugate pairs. Vocabulary: complex number, imaginary number, real part, imaginary part, imaginary unit, complex conjugate, complex plane, modulus, argument, Fundamental Theorem of Algebra.

Mathematics.teks_description: Apply volume formulas for three-dimensional figures in mathematical and real-world problems
Mathematics.prompt: This topic covers modeling with geometry for 10th grade Mathematics students (TEKS G.11(D)). Students should apply volume formulas for three-dimensional figures in mathematical and real-world problems. Volume formulas: prism V = Bh (B = base area), cylinder V = πr^2h, cone V = (1/3)πr^2h, pyramid V = (1/3)Bh, sphere V = (4/3)πr^3. Surface area formulas: prism SA = 2B + Ph (P = perimeter of base), cylinder SA = 2πr^2 + 2πrh, cone SA = πr^2 + πrl (l = slant height), sphere SA = 4πr^2. Key concepts: choosing appropriate formulas, composite figures (add or subtract volumes), density = mass/volume, optimization problems (maximizing volume for given surface area or minimizing surface area for given volume). Real-world applications: packaging design, storage capacity, construction, liquid capacity. Cross-sections: slicing 3D figures creates 2D shapes; identifying cross-sections of prisms, cylinders, cones, spheres, and pyramids. Vocabulary: volume, surface area, prism, cylinder, cone, pyramid, sphere, base area, lateral area, slant height, cross-section, composite figure, density, capacity.

Science.teks_description: Analyze and critique scientific explanations using empirical evidence and logical reasoning in biology
Science.prompt: This topic covers advanced biology concepts for 10th grade Science students (TEKS B.4(A)). Students should analyze and critique scientific explanations using empirical evidence and logical reasoning at an advanced level. Building on 9th grade foundations, 10th grade biology dives deeper into molecular biology, biochemistry, genetics, evolution, and ecology. Students apply scientific thinking to evaluate experimental evidence, distinguish between correlation and causation, and assess the validity of scientific claims. Key concepts: experimental design with appropriate controls, analyzing data using statistical reasoning, evaluating peer-reviewed scientific literature, understanding how scientific knowledge evolves through new evidence. Students should be able to construct and defend scientific arguments, identify logical fallacies in scientific reasoning, and apply the nature of science principles to current biological debates. Vocabulary: empirical evidence, logical reasoning, peer review, replication, validity, reliability, statistical significance, control group, experimental group, bias, scientific literacy, critical analysis. Common misconceptions: science proves things absolutely (science provides evidence-based explanations subject to revision); a theory is just an opinion.

Science.teks_description: Relate functions of carbohydrates, lipids, proteins, and nucleic acids to cellular structure and function
Science.prompt: This topic covers biochemistry for 10th grade Science students (TEKS B.5(A)). Students should relate the functions of biological macromolecules to cellular structure and function. Four major macromolecules: carbohydrates (sugars and starches, quick energy and structural support; monomers: monosaccharides), lipids (fats, oils, waxes; long-term energy storage, cell membrane structure, insulation; not true polymers), proteins (enzymes, structural components, transport, defense; monomers: amino acids linked by peptide bonds), nucleic acids (DNA and RNA; store and transmit genetic information; monomers: nucleotides). Enzymes: biological catalysts that speed up chemical reactions by lowering activation energy; substrate-specific (lock-and-key model or induced fit); affected by temperature, pH, and substrate concentration; denaturation occurs when enzyme shape is altered. Key concepts: dehydration synthesis builds polymers (releases water), hydrolysis breaks polymers (uses water), structure determines function, functional groups determine properties. Vocabulary: macromolecule, monomer, polymer, carbohydrate, lipid, protein, nucleic acid, enzyme, substrate, active site, denaturation, activation energy, dehydration synthesis, hydrolysis, peptide bond, amino acid.

Science.teks_description: Explain the cell cycle significance including DNA replication and mitosis stages
Science.prompt: This topic covers cellular reproduction for 10th grade Science students (TEKS B.6(A)). Students should explain the cell cycle including DNA replication and stages of mitosis. Cell cycle: interphase (G1 growth, S DNA synthesis, G2 preparation) followed by M phase (mitosis + cytokinesis). Mitosis stages: prophase (chromosomes condense, spindle forms), metaphase (chromosomes align at cell equator), anaphase (sister chromatids separate toward poles), telophase (nuclear envelopes reform), cytokinesis (cytoplasm divides). Mitosis result: two genetically identical diploid daughter cells. Meiosis: two divisions producing four genetically unique haploid gametes. Meiosis I: homologous chromosomes separate (crossing over and independent assortment increase genetic diversity). Meiosis II: sister chromatids separate (like mitosis). Key concepts: cell cycle checkpoints and regulation, cancer as uncontrolled cell division, comparison of mitosis and meiosis, significance of genetic diversity in meiosis. Vocabulary: cell cycle, interphase, mitosis, meiosis, cytokinesis, chromosome, chromatid, centromere, spindle, diploid, haploid, homologous, crossing over, independent assortment, gamete.

Science.teks_description: Identify DNA changes and evaluate their significance
Science.prompt: This topic covers genetics for 10th grade Science students (TEKS B.7(C)). Students should identify DNA changes and evaluate their significance. DNA mutations: point mutations (substitution, insertion, deletion of single nucleotides), frameshift mutations (insertions or deletions that shift the reading frame), chromosomal mutations (deletion, duplication, inversion, translocation of chromosome segments). Effects: silent (no amino acid change due to codon degeneracy), missense (different amino acid), nonsense (premature stop codon), frameshift (often severely changes protein). DNA replication errors: most are corrected by proofreading enzymes, but some persist as mutations. Mutagens: agents that increase mutation rate (UV radiation, chemicals, certain viruses). Gene regulation: not all genes are expressed in all cells; regulation occurs at multiple levels (transcriptional, post-transcriptional, translational, post-translational). Key concepts: mutations as source of genetic variation for evolution, beneficial vs. harmful vs. neutral mutations, relationship between genotype and phenotype. Vocabulary: mutation, point mutation, frameshift, substitution, insertion, deletion, mutagen, gene expression, gene regulation, silent mutation, missense, nonsense, chromosomal mutation.

Science.teks_description: Discuss PCR, gel electrophoresis, and genetic engineering applications
Science.prompt: This topic covers molecular biology and biotechnology for 10th grade Science students (TEKS B.7(D)). Students should discuss advanced biotechnology techniques and their applications. Building on 9th grade: deeper understanding of PCR (thermal cycling details, primer design, applications in COVID testing and forensics), gel electrophoresis (DNA fingerprinting, RFLP analysis, forensic identification), genetic engineering (CRISPR-Cas9 gene editing technology, gene therapy for genetic disorders, transgenic organisms). Advanced concepts: genomics (study of entire genomes, Human Genome Project), proteomics (study of all proteins), bioinformatics (computational analysis of biological data), stem cell research and applications. Applications: personalized medicine, pharmacogenomics, agricultural biotechnology, environmental biotechnology (bioremediation), synthetic biology. Ethical dimensions: genetic privacy and discrimination (GINA Act), designer babies debate, GMO labeling, patent issues on genetic material, equitable access to genetic technologies. Vocabulary: PCR, gel electrophoresis, CRISPR, gene therapy, transgenic, genomics, proteomics, bioinformatics, stem cell, recombinant DNA, vector, plasmid, restriction enzyme, bioethics.

Science.teks_description: Analyze how natural selection leads to speciation
Science.prompt: This topic covers evolution for 10th grade Science students (TEKS B.10(C)). Students should analyze how natural selection leads to speciation. Speciation: formation of new species; occurs when populations become reproductively isolated. Types: allopatric speciation (geographic isolation separates populations, e.g., mountain range, river), sympatric speciation (reproductive isolation within same area, e.g., polyploidy in plants, habitat differentiation). Reproductive isolation mechanisms: prezygotic (habitat, temporal, behavioral, mechanical, gametic) and postzygotic (hybrid inviability, infertility, breakdown). Mechanisms of evolution: natural selection, genetic drift (random changes in allele frequency, greater effect in small populations), gene flow (migration introduces alleles), mutation (creates new alleles), sexual selection. Evidence for evolution: fossil record (transitional fossils), comparative anatomy (homologous structures), molecular biology (DNA sequence comparisons, molecular clocks), biogeography, embryology, direct observation (bacterial resistance). Vocabulary: speciation, reproductive isolation, allopatric, sympatric, genetic drift, gene flow, mutation, natural selection, sexual selection, transitional fossil, molecular clock, adaptive radiation, convergent evolution, divergent evolution.

Science.skill: Ecology (energy flow, nutrient cycles, population dynamics)
Science.teks_description: Analyze disruptions to matter cycling and energy flow through trophic levels
Science.prompt: This topic covers ecology for 10th grade Science students (TEKS B.13(B)). Students should analyze disruptions to matter cycling and energy flow through trophic levels. Energy flow: unidirectional through ecosystems; producers convert solar energy to chemical energy, approximately 10% transfers to each successive trophic level, rest lost as heat. Ecological pyramids: energy (always upright), biomass (usually upright), numbers (can be inverted). Biogeochemical cycles: carbon cycle (photosynthesis, respiration, decomposition, combustion, ocean absorption), nitrogen cycle (fixation by bacteria, nitrification, assimilation, denitrification), phosphorus cycle (weathering of rocks, no atmospheric component), water cycle (evaporation, condensation, precipitation, transpiration, runoff). Disruptions: excess CO2 from fossil fuels (enhanced greenhouse effect), nitrogen fertilizer runoff (eutrophication), deforestation (carbon release, habitat loss), pollution. Population dynamics: exponential vs. logistic growth, carrying capacity, density-dependent and density-independent limiting factors. Vocabulary: trophic level, energy pyramid, biomass, biogeochemical cycle, carbon cycle, nitrogen cycle, nitrogen fixation, eutrophication, carrying capacity, limiting factor, ecological footprint.

Science.teks_description: Explain carbon and nitrogen cycle significance and disruption consequences
Science.prompt: This topic covers human impact on ecosystems for 10th grade Science students (TEKS B.13(C)). Students should explain the significance of carbon and nitrogen cycles and consequences of their disruption. Carbon cycle disruptions: burning fossil fuels releases stored carbon as CO2, deforestation reduces carbon sinks, cement production releases CO2. Consequences: enhanced greenhouse effect, global warming, ocean acidification (CO2 dissolves in seawater forming carbonic acid, lowering pH), coral bleaching. Nitrogen cycle disruptions: synthetic fertilizers (Haber-Bosch process) add reactive nitrogen, fossil fuel combustion produces NOx. Consequences: eutrophication (excess nutrients cause algal blooms, decomposition depletes oxygen creating dead zones), acid rain (NOx contributes), groundwater contamination (nitrates in drinking water). Other human impacts: habitat destruction, pollution (air, water, soil), invasive species introduction, overexploitation of resources, biodiversity loss. Solutions: renewable energy, conservation, sustainable agriculture, pollution control, protected areas, international agreements. Vocabulary: carbon sink, carbon source, greenhouse effect, ocean acidification, eutrophication, dead zone, acid rain, nitrogen fixation, denitrification, biodiversity loss, sustainability, conservation.

Science.teks_description: Analyze animal system interactions for regulation, nutrient absorption, reproduction, and defense
Science.prompt: This topic covers human body systems in detail for 10th grade Science students (TEKS B.12(A)). Students should analyze how animal body systems interact for regulation, nutrient absorption, reproduction, and defense at a deeper level. System interactions: circulatory + respiratory (gas exchange at lungs and tissues, oxygen-hemoglobin binding), digestive + circulatory (nutrient absorption in small intestine villi, transport via blood), nervous + endocrine (neuroendocrine integration, hypothalamus-pituitary axis), muscular + skeletal (movement via antagonistic muscle pairs, lever systems). Homeostasis: thermoregulation (nervous system detects, blood vessels dilate/constrict, sweat glands activate), blood glucose regulation (insulin lowers, glucagon raises — negative feedback), blood pressure regulation. Immune system detail: innate (barriers, inflammation, phagocytes) vs. adaptive (B cells/antibodies, T cells/cell-mediated, memory cells). Reproduction: hormonal control of reproductive cycles, fertilization, embryonic development basics. Vocabulary: homeostasis, negative feedback, positive feedback, hormone, receptor, effector, neurotransmitter, synapse, antibody, antigen, B cell, T cell, villi, alveoli, nephron, hemoglobin, antagonistic muscles.

Science.teks_description: Analyze and critique scientific explanations using empirical evidence in chemistry
Science.prompt: This topic covers chemistry foundations for 10th grade Science students (TEKS C.4(A)). Students should apply analytical and critical thinking skills to evaluate chemical phenomena using empirical evidence. At the 10th grade level, chemistry builds on foundational concepts with deeper mathematical analysis and more complex problem-solving. Students should: design experiments with appropriate controls to test chemical hypotheses, analyze quantitative data from chemical investigations, evaluate the validity of chemical models (atomic models, bonding theories), understand how scientific understanding of chemistry has evolved historically (Dalton, Thomson, Rutherford, Bohr, quantum model). Key concepts: distinguishing between observations and inferences, identifying sources of experimental error, understanding precision vs. accuracy, using significant figures correctly, applying dimensional analysis. Students should connect microscopic models (atomic and molecular) to macroscopic observations (physical and chemical properties). Vocabulary: empirical, qualitative, quantitative, precision, accuracy, significant figures, dimensional analysis, model, theory, law, observation, inference, hypothesis, systematic error, random error.

Science.teks_description: Perform calculations involving heat, mass, temperature change, and specific heat
Science.prompt: This topic covers thermochemistry and thermodynamics for 10th grade Science students (TEKS C.13(D)). Students should perform calculations involving heat, mass, temperature change, and specific heat. Core equation: q = mcΔT where q = heat energy (joules), m = mass (grams), c = specific heat capacity (J/g°C), ΔT = temperature change (Tf - Ti). Water's specific heat: 4.184 J/g°C (high compared to most substances). Calorimetry: measuring heat changes using qreleased = -qabsorbed. Exothermic vs. endothermic: exothermic releases heat (ΔH < 0, feels warm), endothermic absorbs heat (ΔH > 0, feels cold). Enthalpy changes: represent energy of reactions; Hess's law allows calculating ΔH for reactions by summing steps. Energy diagrams: show activation energy, reactant/product energy levels, and catalyst effects. Key concepts: conservation of energy, system vs. surroundings, specific heat determines how quickly substances heat up. Vocabulary: thermochemistry, enthalpy, specific heat, calorimetry, exothermic, endothermic, activation energy, catalyst, Hess's law, joule, calorie, heat capacity, system, surroundings.

Science.teks_description: Classify processes as exothermic or endothermic and represent energy changes
Science.prompt: This topic covers chemical kinetics and equilibrium for 10th grade Science students (TEKS C.13(C)). Students should classify processes as exothermic or endothermic and understand reaction energetics. Kinetics: study of reaction rates. Factors affecting rate: temperature (higher = faster), concentration (higher = faster), surface area (more = faster), catalysts (lower activation energy), nature of reactants. Collision theory: effective collisions require sufficient energy and proper orientation. Equilibrium: dynamic state where forward and reverse rates are equal; concentrations remain constant. Le Chatelier's principle: system responds to stress by shifting to counteract it — adding reactant shifts right, removing product shifts right, increasing temperature shifts toward endothermic direction, pressure changes affect gases. Equilibrium expression: Keq = [products]/[reactants] with coefficients as exponents; large Keq favors products, small Keq favors reactants. Thermochemical equations: include ΔH values; can be manipulated (reverse = change sign, multiply = multiply ΔH). Vocabulary: kinetics, rate, collision theory, activation energy, catalyst, equilibrium, Le Chatelier's principle, equilibrium constant, exothermic, endothermic, thermochemical equation, dynamic equilibrium.

Science.teks_description: Analyze substance properties regarding intramolecular and intermolecular forces
Science.prompt: This topic covers introductory organic chemistry for 10th grade Science students (TEKS C.7(D)). Students should analyze substance properties based on intramolecular and intermolecular forces. Carbon chemistry: carbon forms 4 covalent bonds, creating diverse molecular structures (chains, branches, rings). Hydrocarbons: alkanes (single bonds, saturated), alkenes (double bonds), alkynes (triple bonds). Functional groups: hydroxyl (-OH, alcohols), carboxyl (-COOH, carboxylic acids), amino (-NH2, amines), carbonyl (C=O, aldehydes/ketones). Intramolecular forces: covalent bonds within molecules; bond energy, bond length, and polarity depend on atoms involved. Intermolecular forces: forces between molecules determining physical properties; London dispersion (all molecules, increases with molecular size), dipole-dipole (polar molecules), hydrogen bonding (H bonded to F, O, or N — strongest intermolecular force in molecular compounds). Property trends: boiling point, melting point, and viscosity increase with stronger intermolecular forces; solubility follows like dissolves like. Vocabulary: organic, hydrocarbon, functional group, intramolecular, intermolecular, London dispersion, dipole-dipole, hydrogen bond, polarity, saturated, unsaturated, isomer.

Science.teks_description: Describe alpha, beta, and gamma radioactive decay characteristics using balanced nuclear equations
Science.prompt: This topic covers nuclear chemistry for 10th grade Science students (TEKS C.14(A)). Students should describe radioactive decay types and write balanced nuclear equations. Types of radioactive decay: alpha decay (emission of helium-4 nucleus, mass number decreases by 4, atomic number by 2; heavy elements), beta decay (neutron converts to proton + electron; atomic number increases by 1, mass unchanged; neutron-rich nuclei), gamma decay (emission of high-energy photon; no change in mass or atomic number; accompanies other decay types). Balancing nuclear equations: conserve mass number (top) and atomic number (bottom) on both sides. Half-life: time for half of radioactive sample to decay; N = N0(1/2)^(t/t1/2). Nuclear fission: heavy nucleus splits (e.g., U-235), releases large energy, basis of nuclear power. Nuclear fusion: light nuclei combine (e.g., hydrogen to helium), even more energy, powers the sun. Applications: medical imaging and therapy, carbon-14 dating, nuclear power, smoke detectors. Vocabulary: radioactive decay, alpha particle, beta particle, gamma ray, half-life, nuclear equation, fission, fusion, isotope, radioisotope, transmutation, chain reaction, nuclear binding energy.

Science.teks_description: Analyze and critique scientific explanations using empirical evidence in physics
Science.prompt: This topic covers physics foundations for 10th grade Science students (TEKS Phy.4(A)). At the 10th grade level, students build upon introductory physics with more rigorous mathematical treatment and deeper conceptual understanding. Students should analyze and critique physical explanations using quantitative evidence, vector analysis, and mathematical modeling. Key areas of study: advanced kinematics (two-dimensional motion, projectile problems), dynamics (complex force scenarios, inclined planes, friction), energy (conservation with multiple energy types), waves (standing waves, interference), and introduction to electricity and magnetism. Mathematical skills applied: trigonometry for vector decomposition, algebra for multi-step problem solving, graphical analysis for interpreting motion and force diagrams. Students should design experiments, analyze data, calculate percent error, and evaluate whether results support or refute hypotheses. Scientific models: evaluate limitations of physical models and understand when simplifications (frictionless surfaces, point masses) are appropriate. Vocabulary: vector, scalar, component, resolution, model, idealization, percent error, systematic error, experimental design, quantitative analysis.

Science.teks_description: Describe and analyze uniform circular and projectile motion in two dimensions
Science.prompt: This topic covers two-dimensional motion for 10th grade Science students (TEKS Phy.5(D)). Students should describe and analyze uniform circular and projectile motion. Projectile motion: combines constant horizontal velocity (no horizontal acceleration) with vertical free fall (a = g = 9.8 m/s² downward); trajectory is parabolic. Horizontal and vertical components are independent. Key equations: horizontal (x = v0x × t), vertical (y = v0y × t - ½gt², vy = v0y - gt). Launch angle affects range: 45° gives maximum range (no air resistance). Uniform circular motion: constant speed in a circle; velocity direction constantly changes (acceleration exists). Centripetal acceleration: ac = v²/r, directed toward center. Centripetal force: Fc = mv²/r, the net inward force (not a separate force — provided by tension, gravity, friction, etc.). Period: T = 2πr/v. Key concepts: independence of horizontal and vertical motions, time is the linking variable in projectile problems, circular motion is accelerated even at constant speed. Vocabulary: projectile, trajectory, range, launch angle, centripetal acceleration, centripetal force, uniform circular motion, period, frequency, tangential velocity.

Science.teks_description: Calculate force effects using free body diagrams and Newton's second law
Science.prompt: This topic covers applied forces and Newton's laws for 10th grade Science students (TEKS Phy.5(F)). Students should solve complex force problems using free body diagrams and Newton's second law. Advanced applications: inclined planes (resolve gravity into parallel and perpendicular components: F_parallel = mg sin θ, F_perpendicular = mg cos θ), connected objects (multiple objects with tension), Atwood machines (two masses over a pulley), objects in equilibrium (ΣF = 0 in both x and y). Friction: static friction fs ≤ μsN (prevents motion), kinetic friction fk = μkN (opposes motion); calculate normal force on inclined planes as N = mg cos θ. Multi-body problems: draw separate FBDs for each object, write ΣF = ma for each, use constraints (same acceleration for connected objects, same tension in massless rope). Key concepts: choosing optimal coordinate system, handling multiple forces in 2D, applying Newton's laws to real-world scenarios (elevator problems, towing, banking). Vocabulary: free body diagram, normal force, friction, tension, inclined plane, component, net force, equilibrium, coefficient of friction, constraint, system, acceleration.

Science.teks_description: Calculate impulse and momentum in physical systems
Science.prompt: This topic covers momentum and impulse for 10th grade Science students (TEKS Phy.7(D)). Students should calculate impulse and momentum in physical systems. Momentum: p = mv, vector quantity (has direction), measured in kg·m/s. Impulse: J = FΔt = Δp, the change in momentum caused by a force over time. Conservation of momentum: total momentum of an isolated system remains constant (Σpi = Σpf). Collisions: elastic (momentum AND kinetic energy conserved — billiard balls), inelastic (momentum conserved, KE not — car crashes), perfectly inelastic (objects stick together, maximum KE loss). Collision equations: m1v1i + m2v2i = m1v1f + m2v2f (general); m1v1i + m2v2i = (m1+m2)vf (perfectly inelastic). Applications: car safety (airbags and crumple zones increase impact time to reduce force), rocket propulsion (expelling gas creates thrust by conservation of momentum), sports (follow-through increases impulse). Vocabulary: momentum, impulse, conservation of momentum, elastic collision, inelastic collision, perfectly inelastic, isolated system, recoil, kinetic energy, impact force, impulse-momentum theorem.

Science.teks_description: Calculate gravitational force magnitude using Newton's law of universal gravitation
Science.prompt: This topic covers circular motion and gravitation for 10th grade Science students (TEKS Phy.5(H)). Students should calculate gravitational force using Newton's law of universal gravitation. Newton's law of universal gravitation: F = Gm1m2/r², where G = 6.674 × 10^-11 N·m²/kg², m1 and m2 are the two masses, r is the distance between centers. Gravitational field strength: g = GM/r², equals 9.8 m/s² at Earth's surface. Orbital motion: gravitational force provides centripetal force; setting GMm/r² = mv²/r gives orbital velocity v = √(GM/r). Weight varies by location (on Moon, on other planets) but mass stays constant. Key concepts: inverse-square relationship (doubling distance reduces force to 1/4), gravitational force is always attractive, all masses attract all other masses, weightlessness in orbit is really free fall. Kepler's laws: (1) elliptical orbits, (2) equal areas in equal times, (3) T² proportional to r³. Vocabulary: gravitational force, gravitational constant, universal gravitation, gravitational field, orbital velocity, weight, mass, inverse-square law, Kepler's laws, satellite, orbit, free fall, escape velocity.

Science.teks_description: Apply conservation of energy using work-energy theorem and transformations
Science.prompt: This topic covers energy conservation for 10th grade Science students (TEKS Phy.7(C)). Students should apply the work-energy theorem and conservation of energy to solve problems. Work-energy theorem: Wnet = ΔKE = ½mv²f - ½mv²i; net work on an object equals its change in kinetic energy. Conservation of mechanical energy: KE1 + PE1 = KE2 + PE2 (when only conservative forces act); ½mv²1 + mgh1 = ½mv²2 + mgh2. Including non-conservative forces: KE1 + PE1 + Wnc = KE2 + PE2, where Wnc accounts for friction (negative) or applied forces. Energy types: kinetic (½mv²), gravitational potential (mgh), elastic potential (½kx²). Power: P = W/t = Fv, rate of energy transfer (watts). Problem-solving strategy: identify system, choose initial and final states, apply conservation or work-energy theorem. Applications: roller coasters, pendulums, projectiles, springs, sliding with friction. Key concepts: choosing reference point for PE, energy is scalar, energy bar charts as visual tools. Vocabulary: work-energy theorem, conservation of energy, kinetic energy, potential energy, mechanical energy, conservative force, non-conservative force, power, watt, joule, dissipation.

Science.teks_description: Analyze and construct series and parallel circuits using schematics
Science.prompt: This topic covers electricity and magnetism for 10th grade Science students (TEKS Phy.6(D)). Students should analyze and construct series and parallel circuits. Circuit fundamentals: current I = Q/t (charge flow rate, amperes), voltage V (potential difference, volts), resistance R (opposition, ohms). Ohm's law: V = IR. Series circuits: same current through all components, voltages add, resistances add (Req = R1+R2). Parallel circuits: same voltage across branches, currents add, 1/Req = 1/R1 + 1/R2. Power: P = IV = I²R = V²/R. Kirchhoff's laws: junction rule (currents in = currents out), loop rule (voltages around loop sum to zero). Magnetism basics: magnetic fields from magnets and current-carrying wires, electromagnetic induction (changing magnetic field induces current), applications (motors, generators, transformers). Key concepts: circuit diagrams and symbols, measuring with ammeter (series) and voltmeter (parallel), household wiring, electrical safety, relationship between electricity and magnetism. Vocabulary: current, voltage, resistance, Ohm's law, series, parallel, circuit, ammeter, voltmeter, power, magnetic field, electromagnetic induction, Kirchhoff's laws.

Science.teks_description: Compare electromagnetic spectrum applications including radio telescopes and x-rays
Science.prompt: This topic covers electromagnetic waves for 10th grade Science students (TEKS Phy.8(E)). Students should compare applications across the electromagnetic spectrum. Electromagnetic waves: transverse waves consisting of oscillating electric and magnetic fields perpendicular to each other and to propagation direction; travel at c = 3 × 10^8 m/s in vacuum; do not require a medium. EM spectrum (increasing frequency): radio (communication, radio telescopes for astronomy), microwaves (cooking, cell phones, radar), infrared (thermal imaging, night vision, remote controls), visible light (human vision, ~400-700 nm, ROY G BIV), ultraviolet (sterilization, black lights, causes sunburn), X-rays (medical imaging, airport security, crystallography), gamma rays (cancer treatment, food sterilization, nuclear processes). Wave relationships: c = fλ, E = hf (higher frequency = higher energy). Applications in science: radio telescopes detect celestial radio waves, spectroscopy identifies elements by emission/absorption spectra, medical imaging uses X-rays and MRI. Vocabulary: electromagnetic wave, electromagnetic spectrum, frequency, wavelength, radio wave, microwave, infrared, ultraviolet, X-ray, gamma ray, photon, spectroscopy, speed of light.
